description: Open 2C with a hand too strong for a one-level opening: 22+ HCP balanced, or about 9+ playing tricks unbalanced, or a ~4-loser hand; game-forcing except after 2C-2D-2NT
purpose: Reserve the 2C opening for hands too strong to risk being passed out after a one-level start, and install a game-forcing structure (except the 2NT rebid) where opener gets time to describe shape

authority.label: ACBL SAYC — Strong 2C is the standard SAYC game-forcing opening

teaching.tradeoff: Using 2C as a strong artificial opening means you lose the natural 2C bid — but hands with 22+ HCP are rare enough that the tradeoff is overwhelmingly positive.
teaching.principle: Strong hands need space to describe themselves. The 2C opening forces the auction to game, giving opener time to show shape without risk of being passed out.

commonMistakes.text: Do not treat 2D as showing 0-7 HCP specifically.
commonMistakes.reason: In the standard waiting style 2D is bid with any hand that lacks a positive — including balanced 8- or 9-counts that have no genuine 5-card suit — not only the weak hands.

commonMistakes.text: Do not jump into a positive response without suit quality.
commonMistakes.reason: A positive response (2H/2S/3C/3D) promises about 8+ HCP plus a genuine 5+ card suit, typically with two of the top three honors or comparable values — not just any 8-count with five small cards.
The auction is game-forcing once 2C is opened — responder must keep the bidding alive, and pass is legal only after opener's 2NT rebid (22-24 balanced) with a yarborough.

commonMistakes.text: Do not open 2C on pure HCP alone when the hand has no tricks.
commonMistakes.reason: A balanced 22 is standard, but 9+ playing tricks or ~4-loser unbalanced hands also qualify; flat 20-21 counts without a source of tricks should start with 2NT or a one-level opening instead.

explanationEntries.displayText: 22+ HCP or 9+ playing tricks qualifies for strong 2C opening
explanationEntries.contrastiveDisplayText: Does not have 22+ HCP for strong 2C opening
Has 8+ HCP — too strong for waiting response
Fewer than 8 HCP — not strong enough for positive response

explanationEntries.displayText: 8+ HCP, balanced, no 5-card suit — positive 2NT response
explanationEntries.contrastiveDisplayText: Does not qualify for balanced positive 2NT response
Does not have 3-7 HCP game values
Fewer than 8 HCP — not enough for slam try
Fewer than 3 hearts to support partner
Fewer than 3 spades to support partner
Fewer than 3 clubs to support partner
Fewer than 3 diamonds to support partner

explanationEntries.displayText: 3H jump rebid: solid hearts one-suiter with slam interest
3S jump rebid: solid spades one-suiter with slam interest
Stayman 3♣ over 2NT rebid: asks for a 4-card major

definitions.description: 22+ HCP or 9+ playing tricks qualifies for strong 2C opening
2D is the true waiting bid — any hand lacking a suit-quality positive bids 2D regardless of HCP

summaryCard.trigger: You open strong two clubs, partner responds
summaryCard.partnership: Waiting and positive responses should be agreed in advance. The auction is forcing to game except after opener's two-notrump rebid, where responder may pass with a yarborough. TODO: qualification currently requires twenty-two or more high-card points; unbalanced strong hands with playing tricks are not yet captured because no playing-tricks fact exists in the catalog. The Stayman and Jacoby surfaces over opener's two-notrump rebid are re-authored here as a convenience; a later refactor should delegate to the sibling modules instead of duplicating surfaces.

whenToUse.gloss: Use the artificial opening when raw strength already commits the partnership to game.
Use the waiting reply with a hand that lacks a positive action but must keep the forcing auction alive.
Show a real suit at once when responder has extra values and a genuine source of tricks.

workedAuctions.label: Waiting keeps the strong hand hidden

calls.rationale: Opener starts with the artificial game force.
Responder gives the waiting reply, promising nothing about diamonds.
Opener clarifies the strong balanced hand while keeping the game force in place.
Responder can now use Stayman over the notrump rebid to search for a major fit.

workedAuctions.label: Positive suit response sets strain early

calls.rationale: Opener begins with the artificial game force.
Responder shows a positive hand with a real heart suit instead of waiting.
Opener supports the suit and confirms that hearts are now the strain to develop.
Responder settles in game, leaving room for later control bidding if the combined strength warrants it.

workedAuctions.label: Counter-example: too little power forces too high

calls.rationale: This opening is wrong when opener does not really hold the partnership's strong range.
Responder gives the routine waiting reply, assuming opener truly has game-forcing power.
Opener is now forced to keep describing a hand that never belonged in the forcing structure.
The partnership is carried to game on trust, even though the opening never had the strength that made the force safe.

items.ourAction: Most pairs keep waiting and positive responses on, using pass or redouble only by agreement.
items.note: The opening stays artificial and forcing even when the opponents try to disturb it.

items.ourAction: Responder usually doubles to show the waiting hand, while natural suit bids remain positive.
items.note: The goal is still to keep the force alive without losing the ability to show a real suit.

items.recommendation: Bid two diamonds waiting whenever no positive action is available.
items.note: The waiting reply is not capped by HCP; any hand that cannot show a genuine positive — including many balanced hands with opening strength — starts here.

items.recommendation: Show a genuine suit (two hearts, two spades, three clubs, or three diamonds) with a good five-card or longer holding and about eight-plus high-card points.
items.note: Canonical suit quality is two of the top three honors or comparable values; bare length without honor strength is insufficient.

items.recommendation: Keep pushing toward game; the auction is forcing except after opener's two-notrump rebid.
items.note: Only the two-clubs / two-diamonds / two-notrump sequence (showing a twenty-two to twenty-four balanced opener) breaks the game force; there responder may pass with a yarborough.

relatedLinks.discriminator: Other two-level openings are preemptive; this one starts a forcing auction built on power.
Transfer responses belong after the later notrump rebid, not over the strong two clubs opening itself.

teaching.purpose: Open very strong hands (22+ HCP) with an artificial 2C bid that is game-forcing, then describe hand type through rebids
teaching.whenToUse: Open 2C with 22+ HCP. As responder, bid 2D (waiting) with 0-7 HCP, or make a positive response (suit or 2NT) with 8+ HCP. After 2D, opener rebids naturally: suit with 5+, 2NT with 22-24 balanced, 3NT with 25-27 balanced.
whenNotToUse: With 20-21 HCP — open at the 1-level and jump-rebid
With a weak hand and a long suit — use a Weak Two instead
2C is always artificial — never open 2C to show clubs
teaching.tradeoff: 2C loses its natural club meaning in exchange for a forcing opening that prevents being passed out with a monster hand
teaching.principle: The strongest hands need a forcing opening to ensure the auction continues. 2C → 2D (waiting) is the universal relay; opener then describes shape and strength through rebids.
teaching.roles: Opener describes; responder evaluates. After 2D waiting, the auction is game-forcing (except after 2NT rebid with 0-2 HCP).
